---
title: "Political Factors"
---

## Politics and Procurement Discretion

The choice of allocation mechanism is not purely technical — it is also shaped by political incentives. This section documents two political patterns in the data: the **electoral cycle** and **party alignment**.

---

## Electoral Cycle

Local governments are more likely to bypass public auctions in the year **before municipal elections**. The probit model on mechanism choice (public auction = 0, I3P or direct = 1) shows:

::: {.callout-important}
## Electoral cycle finding

Being in the year before municipal elections increases the probability of using a discretionary allocation mechanism by **6.2 percentage points**, controlling for project size, complexity, and state fixed effects.
:::

This pattern is consistent with **electoral-motivated contracting**: governments distribute contracts to politically connected firms ahead of elections. The effect is especially pronounced in municipalities governed by the PRI.

### Excess Costs Rise Near Elections

The electoral cycle also affects cost efficiency in **public** auctions — even when the mechanism itself is competitive:

- Firm excess costs in public auctions are **13.8% higher** in the year before elections
- This suggests political pressure distorts bidding behavior even within competitive auctions
- One interpretation: connected firms bid less aggressively, expecting future rewards regardless of auction outcomes

```{=html}
<div style="background:#E8EDF5; border-left:4px solid #F2A900; border-radius:0 6px 6px 0; padding:1.2em 1.5em; margin:1.5em 0;">
  <strong style="color:#012169;">Mechanism:</strong>
  <p style="margin:0.5em 0 0; color:#444; font-size:0.93em;">
    In election years, firms that expect future direct contracts may shade their bids upward in public auctions — reducing competitive pressure and raising the government's procurement costs. The structural model captures this as higher markup over cost.
  </p>
</div>
```

---

## Party Alignment

Municipal governments that share a party affiliation with the state government face different incentive structures when allocating contracts.

::: {.callout-note}
## Party alignment result

Municipalities governed by **PAN** are **18.9 percentage points less likely** to use discretionary allocation mechanisms than PRI-governed municipalities, all else equal.
:::

This finding is consistent with institutional explanations: PAN municipalities, at least during 2011–2018, faced stronger internal party pressure toward procedural compliance, or had less access to the political networks that facilitated direct contracting.

Additionally:

- When the **municipal and state government share a party**, firm excess costs fall by approximately **9.8%** in public auctions — suggesting that aligned governments may coordinate more efficiently, or face less political pressure to protect connected contractors

---

## Probit Results: Mechanism Choice

The table below summarizes the marginal effects from the probit model predicting whether a contract bypasses a public auction (I3P or direct allocation = 1).

| Predictor | Marginal Effect | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Concrete volume (1 SD increase) | −11.4 pp | Larger projects → more likely to use public auction |
| Sewage work dummy | −9.8 pp | Complex projects → more likely to use public auction |
| Election year (year before municipal election) | **+6.2 pp** | Election pressure → more discretionary allocation |
| PAN municipality (vs. PRI) | **−18.9 pp** | PAN → more likely to use public auction |

All effects are evaluated at the sample mean. Standard errors clustered at the municipality level.

---

## What This Means

The political economy findings complement the cost-efficiency results:

1. **Discretion is selectively misused.** Governments bypass auctions more often near elections, when political motivations are strongest. This is precisely when cost-efficiency is least likely to drive the selection.

2. **Even competitive auctions are affected.** Higher excess costs in public auctions during election years suggest that the distortions reach beyond the choice of mechanism — they affect competitive behavior within auctions.

3. **Party institutions matter.** The large PAN–PRI difference suggests that reducing discretionary contracting may require institutional reforms at the party level, not just legal restrictions on procurement mechanisms.

```{=html}
<div class="finding-card neutral-card" style="margin-top:1.5em;">
  <div class="finding-label">Policy Implication</div>
  <div class="finding-headline">Procurement reform should target election-period contracting</div>
  <p class="finding-body">The results suggest that a targeted rule — requiring public auctions for simple pavement contracts in the year before elections — could substantially reduce procurement costs without restricting legitimate use of discretion for complex projects.</p>
</div>
```
